findings.description: The PDF is a single-page map figure with complex spatial layout including map labels, data callouts, legend, notes, and metadata all arranged spatially around a central map. The HTML converts this into a long linear/vertical layout, fundamentally changing the spatial arrangement from a map-centric figure to a scrollable document.
findings.evidence: PDF shows a single map figure with sample data callouts positioned geographically around the map, legend in bottom-right, notes in bottom-left, and title/metadata on the right side. HTML renders all content as a top-to-bottom linear flow: title, small map thumbnail, then map labels list, then sample data tables, then legend, then notes, then document metadata.

findings.description: The map image in the HTML is rendered as a small thumbnail that loses significant legibility compared to the full-page PDF map. Details, labels, road names, and spatial relationships visible in the PDF map are not discernible in the HTML version's small image.
findings.evidence: PDF shows a large, detailed map occupying most of the page with readable road names, property boundaries, and sample locations. HTML shows a small thumbnail image where these details are not legible.

findings.description: The sample data tables in the PDF are presented as compact callout boxes with the sample ID as a header, containing contaminant names and values in a two-column mini-table format. In the HTML, these are rendered as separate full-width tables, which is functionally acceptable but the Risk Assessment sub-sections within each sample are separated into distinct visual blocks rather than being part of the same callout.
findings.evidence: PDF shows e.g. 'SW-1/DUP-6102025-SD' with Copper 4.69, Lead 0.868 J, then Risk Assessment CR: 2.4E-08, HI: 0.39 all in one compact callout. HTML separates these into table rows but maintains the data correctly. The structure is preserved despite visual differences.

findings.description: The HTML adds a 'Map Labels' section listing SW/SD-1 through SW/SD-5 that doesn't appear as a discrete section in the PDF. These labels exist on the map but are not called out as a separate list.
findings.evidence: PDF has these labels only on the map itself. HTML creates a separate 'Map Labels' section listing them explicitly before the sample data tables.

findings.description: HTML introduces fabricated section headings not present in the PDF. The PDF is a single figure/map with callout boxes and notes. The HTML adds headings like 'Map Labels', 'Sample Data', 'Legend', 'Notes & References', and 'Document Metadata' that do not exist as headings in the source document.
findings.evidence: PDF has no section headings — it is a map figure with callout boxes, a legend, and notes. HTML creates h3 headings: 'Map Labels', 'Sample Data', 'Legend', 'Notes & References', 'Document Metadata'. These are organizational labels invented by the converter.

findings.description: The sample location labels (SW/SD-1 through SW/SD-5) appear redundantly under the fabricated 'Map Labels' heading, duplicating information already conveyed by the tables and the map legend.
findings.evidence: HTML has a section listing 'SW/SD-1, SW/SD-2, SW/SD-3, SW/SD-4, SW/SD-5' under 'Map Labels' heading. These are map annotation labels that are also represented in the table data and legend. In the PDF they appear only as labels on the map.

findings.description: The h2 heading 'SOUTHSIDE PARK LANDFILL' is presented as if it is a subtitle in the HTML, but in the PDF it appears in the title block alongside other metadata, not as a standalone heading. This is a minor structural reinterpretation rather than content fabrication.
findings.evidence: PDF title block contains 'SOUTHSIDE PARK LANDFILL' as part of the figure metadata. HTML elevates it to an h2 heading.
